text:
This study explores Polish–Moldovan relations in the years 1492–1538, from the accession of John Albert to the rule of prince Petru Rareş and to the very moment when Moldova was completely subordinated to the Ottoman Empire. On that occasion the longstanding dispute for Pokuttya was settled and a direct frontier between Poland and Turkey was established, in Budjak taken by the Turks. Based on the research on the reigns of John Albert, Alexander and Sigismund I Old, it ascertains Moldova’s position within the system of relations between: Poland, Hungary, the Ottoman Empire and the Grand Duchy of Moscow.

The book is based on a large international research project using materials from archives in Germany, Austria, Romania, Moldova, Hungary, Poland and Russia. The central idea is to shed new light on several major events in the history of the time. Thanks to an extraordinary discovery of previously unknown sources from the Congress of Levoča we get a completely new and unprecedented view of the preparation of the ill-fated journey of the Prince of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania, Jan Olbracht in 1497. We also get an extraordinary look at the border clashes and diplomatic activities of the most important family of the time – the Chodecki family. Another essential aspect is the complex political activity of the ruler of Moldova, Petru Rareş and thanks to a geopolitical and legal analysis, we get an unprecedented view of the unique position of Moldova as the key element of the system of the European power politics in the period of the transition from the Middle Ages to modern times.
